does: This paper introduces Curriculum Group Policy Optimization (CGPO), an adaptive training framework that improves text-to-image generation by implementing curriculum learning within reinforcement learning. The method builds on Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO) by replacing uniform sampling with adaptive prompt selection during training. The core innovation uses the variance of reward scores across generated images as an online proxy for prompt difficulty or inconsistency, identifying prompts where the model has partially but not fully mastered the requirements. CGPO prioritizes these moderately difficult prompts during training, filtering out both trivially easy prompts (low variance) and impossibly hard ones (high variance). The framework continuously adapts as the model learns, progressively shifting focus toward harder prompts as earlier ones become easier, thereby improving training efficiency and ultimately image quality.
connects: This work extends reinforcement learning approaches to text-to-image generation, building directly on recent GRPO-based methods that have shown promise in this domain. It responds to a well-established problem in machine learning: that uniform sampling of training data is inefficient compared to adaptive curricula. The paper connects to classical curriculum learning literature, which demonstrates that gradually increasing task difficulty matches human learning patterns and improves convergence. It also relates to research on sample prioritization and active learning in deep learning, where identifying samples that the model is poised to learn from most effectively is crucial. The use of variance as a difficulty metric connects to prior work on uncertainty estimation and self-paced learning, applying these principles specifically to the multi-image generation setting enabled by GRPO's group-based approach.
enables: This framework opens several directions for improving reinforcement learning in generative models. The online variance-based difficulty estimation could be adapted to other domains using group-based RL methods, from video generation to 3D synthesis. The work suggests future research into more sophisticated curriculum strategies that combine multiple difficulty metrics or leverage other distributional properties of rewards beyond variance. It enables investigation of how curriculum pacing schedules affect final model quality and training speed, potentially informing optimal learning curves for different model scales and prompt distributions. The approach also raises questions about how curriculum strategies interact with other training improvements like better reward models or different policy optimization variants, setting up ablation studies and meta-learning directions that could optimize the curriculum strategy itself across different domains.
